data.title: 17 Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Buys From 30 Of 73 November Graham Value All-Stars (GVAS)
data.description:
Foreword

About Large Cap Value

“A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during their stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1s are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios.” -YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, seventeen of the thirty “safer” lowest-priced dogs of the GASV are buyable.

December found Noah Holdings (NOAH), Alliance Resource (ARLP), Westlake Chemical (WLKP), IRSA Inversiones (IRS), Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX LP (MPLX), Global Ship (GSL), Altria (MO), Verizon (VZ), SunCoke Energy (SXC), DHT Holdings (DHT), Kilroy Realty (KHC), Ennis (EBF), FinVolution (FINV), AT&T (T), JD.com (JD), and Banco Bilbao (BBVA) on the buy-list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced December GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all fourteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 36.21% To 98.4% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By December, 2026

Eight of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 80% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target-prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to December, 2026 were:

Hafnia (HAFN) was projected to net $983.97 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 743% opposite the market as a whole.

The RMR Group (RMR) was projected to net $699.74 based on the median of target prices estimated by 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% under the market as a whole.

IRSA Inversiones was projected to net $489.36 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 52% over the market as a whole.

Noah Holdings was projected to net $472.57, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 14% under the market as a whole.

SunCoke Energy was projected to net $455.73 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% under the market as a whole.

Westlake Chemical was projected to net $423.80, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 39% less than the market as a whole.

Alliance Resource Partners was projected to net $420.41, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 70% under the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer was projected to net $396.75, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% under the market as a whole.

TORM Plc (TRMD) was projected to net $376.43 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 98% under the market as a whole.

Ennis was projected to net $362.12, based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 61% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 50.81% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 0.45% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 50 December, 2025 GASV By 1yr Total Returns

Top 50 December, 2025 GASV By Analyst Target Gains

Top 50 December, 2025 GASV By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top December GASV Stocks By Yield Ranged 8.16% To 12.74% Per YCharts

Top ten GASV came from four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Four energy members led the top ten, placing first, fifth, sixth, and tenth: TORM Plc [1]; Hafnia Ltd) [5]; Alliance Resource Partners [6]; Energy Transfer [10].

Three financial services stalwarts placed second, fourth, and eighth, Noah Holdings [2], Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) [4], and Ares Capital (ARCC) [8].

Thereafter, two real estate representatives placed third and ninth, The RMR Group (RMR) [3], and IRSA Inversiones [9].

Finally, One materials firm placed seventh, Westlake Chemical [7], to complete the December Dogs of the GASV by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten November GASV by dividend and price as of market close 12/22/2025.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield showed less than ideal status. The prices of all ten are below the projected annual dividends from $1k invested. Yes, all ten are IDEAL. But not all are “safer.” Several are paying dividends with borrowed money.

Costs per $1 of Dividend

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. That condition was reached by forty of the sixty-four highest yield December GASV.

The 50 forward looking dividend reporting stocks in the December GASV Costs Per $1 of dividend ranged from $7.85 for TORM Plc, to $25.35 for EOG Resources Inc.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Fourteen of Sixty-four Dividend Paying GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash. Fourteen GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy. They are paying dividends from borrowed money

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own it. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead: dread the Fed, the toll of trading terms, wars, tacos, and uncertainty.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed for Ten Top Yield GASV “Fair-Priced” For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from that top chart show that all ten are more than 50% away from having a share price greater than divided paid from $1k invested..

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 25.9% To 88.32% Top Ten GASV Upsides To December 2025; (32) No Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 21.77% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of December 22, 2026

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 12/22/25 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors. Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care, Industrials Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 57.25% Vs. (35) 47.01% Net Gains by All Ten Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 21.77% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The very lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Hafnia Ltd showed top analyst-estimated gains of 98.40%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for December were Hafnia Ltd, Noah Holdings, Golub Capital, RMR Group, Irsa Inversiones, with prices ranging from $5.31 to $15.35.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of December 22 were: Energy Transfer; Westlake; TORM; Ares; & Alliance Resource, with prices ranging from $16.30 to $23.35.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the football and are-year buzz euphoria dies and the feckless Tech-fueled market inflation and dithering presidential deals gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of the few hot stocks and memes, look for more GASV to become fair-priced… slow and sure, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 17 Ideal of 30 ‘Safer' December GASV Dogs

Late-December 2025, 30 of the 70 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 12/22/25 data showed 17 of those were fair-priced and IDEAL to buy at this time.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced “Break-Even” Price Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend GASV conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those ten ideal dogs were: Noah Holdings, Alliance Resource, Westlake, IRSA, Energy Transfer, MPLX, Global Ship, Altria, Verizon, and SunCoke.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

All ten share prices of these “safer” survivors are below the dividend payouts from $1k invested. The top chart reveals all ten prices would need to rise more than 10% to exceed the dividend payout from $1k invested.

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as “good” as they can get.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher GASV updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" and “Ideal” GASV dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

data.title: When The Music Stops: 2026 Market Outlook, The Tragedy Of The Consumption Commons
data.description:
Thesis

There’s a great line in “Margin Call,” a movie covering the hours leading into the 2008 financial crisis, when CEO John Tuld explains, "I'm here to guess what the music might do a week, a month, a year from now. That's it. Nothing more. And standing here tonight, I'm afraid that I don't hear a thing. Just... silence." Once the music stops, you see, the party’s over, the bull run is coming to an end.

In a sense, that’s how I look at making long-term predictions. The most important question for 2026, quite simply, is whether the music will stop, thus ending our bull run. As of now, I’d put the chances of that at about 55% in 2026. That number is so close to the coin flip that it’s essentially useless in and of itself. Yet the important thing for investors, by my estimates, is pinning down the potential red flags that could hint at trouble ahead. If an investor can spot these red flags and act on them, it could prevent a lot of heartache.

While I have serious concerns about the overall health of the economy, I’m assigning a “buy” rating for the S&P 500 for 2026. Why? If you exit the market too soon, you can miss lots of upside. Further, even if a recession lurks somewhere beyond the horizon, it may not materialize for months or even years.

As for specific numbers for 2026, the S&P 500 so far has gained about 16% over the past year. Even if the beat keeps playing, I believe we’ll see the tempo slow this year. Thus I project the S&P 500 growing by about 10%, meaning it’ll close the next year around 7,700. However, investors should keep an eye on key developing trends and red flags that may portend a downturn. As such, I’m going to take a dive into some factors and metrics investors should pay especially close attention to in 2026 and beyond.

Not all investors enjoy watching the economy, indicators, and megatrends, but especially in today’s complicated market, I believe it is essential to do so. Crucially, consumer consumption is now the key driver of the global economy, and I suspect that various trends, including the adoption of AI and other automating technologies, a stagnant job market, and inflation, could all threaten this vital economic engine in 2026 and beyond. Investors should keep a very close eye on this consumer consumption engine, as I believe it is now under threat.

In the next few sections, I’m going to take a peek at political economic theory to explain why I believe the consumer consumption engine is under threat and why that is such a major threat to investors. After that, I’ll go through factors investors should monitor for signs that consumer consumption is under threat. (Simply monitoring consumer spending isn’t good enough, as I will explain later.)

Often, when I get theoretical, I have no problem suggesting readers jump ahead to get into the nuts and bolts if the theory isn’t interesting. I do believe this particular theoretical argument is important not just for my thesis, but also for investors in general. A few minutes of your time today might help you understand the beats we dance to and thus might help you anticipate what might make the music stop.

An Ethical Foundation For Economics

Modern economics, as founded by Adam Smith, was actually rooted in ethics. Smith wasn’t an economist, per se, having come before the field was founded. He was a social philosopher and ethicist, and much of his work via The Wealth of Nations was less about finance and more about how people can make ends meet and what could be the foundations of a prosperous society. With that in mind, ethics has a lot to say about modern economics, which is why I’m turning to a specific ethics tool, the so-called “Tragedy of the Commons,” to discuss my prediction for 2026 and beyond.

The Tragedy of the Commons framework was popularized by Garrett Hardin, and it highlights how the pursuit of self-interest can lead to the collapse of shared, unregulated resources, or the so-called “Commons.” Hardin asked readers to envision an open and totally unregulated pasture that multiple shepherds depend on to raise and sustain their livestock. Pursuing their own interest, the shepherds continually increase the size of their herd, adding animals for which they secure essentially the full benefit (+1).

There’s just one problem: the pasture is finite and can support only so many animals. As the animals graze, the commons depletes. Yet the shepards bear only a fraction of this “negative component” for every added animal. We can symbolize this negative component as -1, and crucially, an individual shepherd will bear only a fraction of this, say -0.2. As a result, the shepherds continuously add animals as doing so is in their immediate self-interest, even as the Common pasture is put at risk of collapse. Even if a shepherd recognizes the impending collapse and realizes it could lead to ruin, it’s still in their interest to add animals, because if not, another shepherd will continue to add animals anyway, and collapse remains all but inevitable. This means the prescient shepherd will still lose out in the long run, so in the short run it’s better to cash in.

Ultimately, the Tragedy of the Commons questions a core tenet of modern orthodox economics that the pursuit of self-interest leads to positive outcomes. At least in some cases, pursuing self-interest may lead to negative (often long-term) outcomes for both society as a whole and rational actors, including businesses and investors. In the real world, we’ve seen Commons collapse, with fishing grounds among the most prominent examples. Throughout history, fishing grounds have been overexploited and have collapsed precisely because regulating and/or privatizing them is difficult. We’ve also seen this with buffalo in the United States when early settlers killed off the buffalo to the point of near extinction.

Now, I contend that consumer consumption is at risk of becoming a Commons also prone to collapse, and believe that throughout 2026 (and beyond), investors need to keep a very close eye on overall Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption (GACC). This doesn't mean you should exit the market now, but instead consider exit strategies and prepare to go into a defensive position quickly.

Viewing Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption As a Collapsible Commons

Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption (GACC) has evolved into a Commons that businesses and economies the world over rely upon. While Hardin’s pasture is perfectly open, in the real world, many workers (and their incomes) are protected by trade barriers, vast distances, labor laws (e.g. minimum wage), unions, and various other things. At the same time, in a highly globalized world, generally declining trade barriers have made it far easier for companies from pretty much everywhere to tap into consumers and their consumption the world over. Ditto for businesses looking for more affordable labor. These conditions may make collapse more likely by increasing exposure for consumers and incomes.

In the United States, consumer consumption makes up about 68% of the GDP. This consumption sustains not only local businesses, like restaurants and hair salons, but also companies in places like China and Vietnam that export consumer goods, including toys, electronics, clothing, and more. Globally, the World Bank reports that household final consumption expenditure accounts for 56% of the global GDP in 2023. Government spending is another major economic driver, and it should be noted that much of government spending is also spent on consumers in the form of education, healthcare, etc.

Most consumers, in turn, depend on their wages to fuel their consumption, and herein lies an inherent contradiction that could make GACC a Commons prone to collapse: While consumers rely on wages to sustain the spending that powers the global economy, businesses are incentivized to reduce spending on labor as much as possible while maintaining desired outputs. Doing so can increase profits and reward shareholders, but at the same time, cutting spending on labor could reduce Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption by denying workers the income they need in order to consume.

Consider Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), which is aiming to cut its labor force in half at least through automation and AI. Amazon can also replace high-paying jobs in expensive locales, like the United States, with lower-paying jobs via offshoring. If Amazon uses AI and automation to eliminate ten $100,000 per year jobs, the global economy could lose as much as $1,000,000 in consumer consumption power. Perhaps Amazon then creates two overseas jobs paying $20,000 to oversee the AI and automation tools used to eliminate those jobs, thus generating as much as $40,000 in consumer consumption globally, which is good, but still well short of the $960,000 potentially lost.

When Amazon lays off workers, they could be impacted by declining consumer demand. Former employees might simply not have as much money to spend, including at Amazon. Yet none of those laid-off workers would have been spending their entire income at Amazon. They’d also have been spending it on streaming subscriptions, gas, clothing, eating out, etc. In other words, the lost consumption would be spread across society. While Amazon reaps the full benefits of its labor cost cuts, it will incur only a portion of the lost consumption. Also, like our prescient shepherd discussed earlier, if Amazon were to pivot and try to use less automated technologies in order to preserve jobs and incomes, it could quickly come under pressure as other companies, say Walmart, pursue technology-driven productivity gains. As a result, Amazon and many other companies may end up stuck in a negative feedback loop.

The above boils down many complex processes into a rather simplistic thought experiment. I’m not here to prove that Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption is already collapsing. Still, we can’t ignore the possibility of it unfolding given that it could cause a major recession or depression. At the very least, the Tragedy of the Commons framework suggests that such a collapse is logically plausible. Going into 2026 and beyond, investors should keep a close eye on consumer consumption and factors that could impact it, like the labor market, adoption of AI, so on and so forth. In the next section, I’ll break down various factors investors should monitor.

Warning Signs The Music Might Stop

I suspect we’ll see consumer consumption soften in the months ahead, but that doesn’t necessarily mean a full collapse is in the cards. That said, if AI truly does eliminate as many jobs as feared, human civilization will trend towards a collapse of Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption. The risks are real, but that doesn’t mean the outcome is already ordained. Even if my most cynical views prove correct, that doesn’t mean that a collapse of the consumption commons will occur in 2026. It could come later, in say 2027 or 2032.

Not particularly helpful for investors, right? The “good” news is that I believe there are several trends and indicators we can monitor to measure risks and hopefully spot an impending collapse before markets sink.

Personal Consumption Expenditures and Consumer Debt

The most obvious thing to watch is consumer spending through a measurement like Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE). With consumer spending, by the time we see a significant drop, markets will probably already be in retreat. Fortunately, various indicators may warn of an impending consumer spending slowdown before it fully impacts PCE numbers.

Crucially, measurements like Personal Consumption Expenditure include purchases made with credit. In turn, credit allows consumers to sustain spending even if their income is insufficient or in decline. Consider that Buy Now Pay Later and other credit programs were particularly popular during the 2025 holiday season, with BNPL programs seeing a 9% jump in spending compared to last year. PayPal has seen its BNPL program jump by nearly 25% in terms of spending (ibid.). Holiday shoppers also seem more deliberate, avoiding impulse buys and focusing on finding the best deals, which also suggests finances are tight (ibid.).

An intense focus on deals may be a sign of financial duress, although the scale of that duress is debatable and hard to pin down. Taking on debt isn’t the end of the world, but it is something to monitor. If the current tight financial environment for families proves transitional and wages start growing rapidly, the tight times should pass. However, if consumers continue to lean into debt, and worse, start to fall behind on payments, it bodes ill. We’re seeing rising defaults and delinquencies on car loans, mortgages, and subprime credit cards, but so far it’s more a trickle than a flood. If these metrics worsen, investors should take note.

Cash-Tight Consumers Depressing Corporate Profits

During the opening months of the global tariff war, in the United States, companies absorbed about 51% of the cost of tariffs, passing along about 37% to consumers, while suppliers covered about 9%. Companies weren’t doing this out of generosity, but instead because they weren’t able to pass on price increases. That’s a win for consumers in many ways, yet also further illustrates that consumers are under pressure. Fortunately, earnings have overall remained solid, but going forward, margins and profits will come under more substantial, sustained pressure. Companies may need to cannibalize their profits to sustain their sales.

The good news is that over the past few years, companies have been recording record profits, giving breathing room in some ways. Yet markets have priced those profits in, and valuation metrics, like rising aggregate PE ratios, suggest that many investors expect profits to continue to rise. There’s a fair chance we’ll see the opposite going forward. Margins may shrink, especially if businesses continue to struggle to pass on costs to consumers. If so, we can expect markets to take a substantial hit even if the GDP continues to expand. This, in turn, could force companies to try to boost profits, say by cutting labor costs, which could set off a negative feedback loop leading to layoffs, and eventually, further pressure on consumers, their income, and their consumption.

The Labor Market Is More Important Than GDP Growth

Perhaps the most widely cited economic metric is GDP growth. Traditionally, high GDP growth has correlated with low unemployment and robust consumer consumption as demand for labor becomes tight and wages rise. When the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, it spurs lending as companies use cheap cash to invest in economic activities.

Yet Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has noted that we may be experiencing jobless growth despite rate cuts. Other analysts have echoed this sentiment, and some believe jobless growth could become the norm going forward. Why? Rather than hiring new employees to expand production and output, companies may invest in artificial intelligence and other automating technologies. In fact, because AI and automation are so expensive, low interest rates may encourage investments in automating technologies by making it cheaper to borrow funds to invest.

So far, in the United States, the labor market has remained tepid in spite of rate cuts and strong economic growth, and we can’t rule out AI job loss. Investors need to keep a close eye on the labor market, monitoring not just the standard U-3 unemployment rate (U.S.) but also the U-6 and other metrics. As shown below, the U-6 unemployment rate is rising, and even that metric may understate things. More laid-off workers are turning to gig work to tide themselves over, which keeps them from inflating unemployment metrics. Yet at the same time, gig work replaces only 50 to 65% of the lost income, leaving people in a financially tough spot.

It’s also crucial to keep an eye on college graduates as they’ve been struggling to find career-track jobs, with AI cited as a cause in some fields. MIT has found that 11.7% of jobs in the United States can already be replaced by AI, many of them entry-level. Recent college graduates traditionally haven’t been big spenders even in the best of times, so even if college grads are struggling more so than past generations, the immediate impact on the economy may not be obvious or severe. Yet as young consumers age, they may continue to face tight finances, and this should become increasingly evident in PCE and other metrics.

Ultimately, I believe that continued jobless growth could be an early warning sign that automating technologies are paving the way to collapse. GDP growth, meanwhile, may tell little about the overall state of consumers and their consumption habits.

Cuts To Streaming Services and Highly Discretionary Middle-Class Spending

One of the more interesting developments I observed in 2025 was a shift in the behaviors of fellow pizza connoisseurs when they curtailed delivery and started picking up in stores to save on fees. For most consumers, value-added services like delivery are highly discretionary, which means they can quickly end up on the chopping block when times are tight. McDonald’s CEO, meanwhile, has cited a two-tier economy with lower-income consumers tightening their belts and emphasizing value.

Some of the more concrete red flags I believe investors should keep an eye on are discretionary subscriptions like Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) and Spotify (NYSE:SPOT), which are easy to cancel and thus offer an easy way to save money. Interestingly, while Netflix has reported on subscription numbers in the past, it has stopped doing so with recent quarterly reports. Revenues are growing, which probably suggests subscriptions are still growing, but we can’t say for certain. Netflix could simply be doing a better job of monetizing current customers, and other factors may be in play. Spotify does report subscriber numbers and has enjoyed strong growth over the past several quarters. Even if you’re not interested in Spotify as an investment, I suggest keeping an eye on subscriber numbers. Ditto for other companies that report subscriber growth.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, as far as my buy recommendation is concerned, I am banking on catching early red flags should tragedy start to unfold. I may be underestimating markets and overestimating my abilities (and luck). If so, I could end up getting burned, along with many others. Further, other developments, say the outbreak of a major hot war or trade war, could sink markets, and I may not see such events coming.

As for the collapse of consumer consumption, we should remember that people have fretted over technology-driven job loss for quite some time. Indeed, in the 19th century, Luddites infamously smashed textile machinery, fearing technological job loss. Yet new jobs were created, employment and wealth rose, and by and large, the Luddites were proven wrong. This could happen here as well. AI and other technological advances may create jobs that we’re currently not anticipating. It’s also possible that AI will largely turn out to be fool's gold, failing to deliver on anticipated productivity gains. For us human workers, that might be a good thing.

Key Takeway: March To The Beat, But Be Prepared To Run

In my opinion, investors should always keep a close eye on the larger macro economy. That said, now’s the time to double down and monitor the economy, and particularly consumer consumption, even more closely. Other developments could cause a recession, so investors need to be careful not to develop tunnel vision. However, by my estimates, a collapse of Global Aggregate Consumer Consumption is the biggest threat through the near future. If red flags emerge, investors should consider defensive actions.

For the record, technological progress is inevitable, and trying to stop or slow AI would probably be counterproductive. Ditto for other automating technologies and offshoring too. Offshoring, in particular, can spread wealth globally, which in and of itself strikes me as a good thing. Yet if AI-driven job loss does occur, humanity may need to collectively evaluate how society is organized.

While I covered various metrics in this article, I mostly only touched upon them. Many of the considerations here have been expanded upon in previous articles of mine. Further, none of the above is exhaustive. Going forward, I’ll continue to keep an eye on things, so if you don’t feel like monitoring metrics and the economy yourself, feel free to check back. And best of luck in 2026 and beyond.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 9.25% (December 2025)
data.description:
Introduction

After an extended rally and making fresh all-time highs for several months, the market stalled somewhat in early December. Since then, it has been trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6900. It is not far from the psychologically important level of SPX 7000. There is obviously a tug of war going on between the bulls and some prominent bears right now.

The most recent push to bullish sentiment was provided by the December 10th interest rate cut by the Fed, though it was already widely expected. However, it is not difficult to see the markets cross SPX 7000 by the time the year is out, despite some concerns of an AI-led bubble.

In this environment, obviously, the technology and high-growth funds have disproportionately benefited. The closed-end funds that have exposure to equities of high-growth and technology names have done better than others. But at the same time, some other sectors have also performed well, such as Utility funds, MLPs, and some foreign country-specific funds.

Going into 2026, the further progress and direction of the market may now depend on the actions of the Fed. Also, there is some risk of a mild recession as well, especially if the housing market remains in doldrums. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, it is always hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.10%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally very stressful and full of pitfalls, and that's why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the "5 Best DGI stocks." You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 9.25% (as of 12/19/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 15.6%, 11.6%, and 11.9% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds:

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 12/19/2025.)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years)

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings)

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list by coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 26 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: AIO, CET, CSQ, EFT, EMO, EOD, GAM, JCE, and UTF (9 duplicates).

Appeared three times: EOS, GGN, PDX, STK, TWN, VVR, and XFLT (14 duplicates).

Appeared four times: EDF (3 duplicates).

Once we remove 26 duplicate rows, we are left with 49 (75- 26) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors, like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

Current distribution rate

Excess discount/premium

3-YR NAV return

5.0

5-YR NAV return

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR Total return
