If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

5.0

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

7.5

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, such as equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 49 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_DEC_2025.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month:

Before we get to the final ten, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month

In the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(CCD), (EOS), (PDX), (EOD), (EIC), (RNP), (AIO), (UTF), (ARDC), and (GAM).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on many of our selections:

(CCD) Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income:

The Fund primarily invests in a portfolio of convertible securities, debt securities, and other income-producing securities, including some equities. Investments include common and preferred stocks, investment-grade and below-investment-grade bonds, loans, equity-linked notes, and floating-rate securities. It also uses high leverage exceeding 30%. It pays a monthly distribution that currently yields roughly 11%. The premium is almost insignificant currently, but it compares favorably with its very high premiums over the last 12 months and 3 years. The lower interest rates expected in the future should help this fund. However, on the negative side, there is some chance that the management may reduce the distribution payout as it has a negative income from operations (NII + realized and unrealized capital gains). So, investors need to be somewhat cautious and use due diligence.

(EOS) Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II:

Similar to most months, we had a number of covered-call funds appear in our top 50. We selected two of them, EOS and Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK), for our list of top 20, but we kept EOS for the top 10.

EOS has a very high exposure to the technology sector with nearly 50% allocation, and its top holdings include most of the so-called "Magnificent Seven" and high-growth names. It currently holds only about 50 companies and overwrites (sells options) roughly 50% of its portfolio to generate income and reduce volatility. The distribution yield is attractive at 8.1%, and currently offers a decent discount of -4.3%. The fund uses no leverage, and its expenses are comparable to those of its competitors. The past performance record is solid, and it has returned 23%, 12% and 13.4% (annualized) over the last 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

(PDX) PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund:

This month, in the top 10, we include one of PIMCO's newest but popular funds. This fund regularly appears near the top in multiple categories. The reason it stands out is that it has performed remarkably well in the last three and five years. The fund is only about six years old, so it does not have a long history. Some of its past performance can be attributed to its timing of inception (in 2019) and initial allocation to the energy sector. In fact, this fund was known as "PIMCO Energy & Tactical Credit Opportunities fund (ticker - NRGX)" until Nov.2023. Since the beginning of 2024, PIMCO has decided to diversify the fund from an energy-focused fund to a mix of energy and multi-sector Credit/Bonds. Unlike other PIMCO funds, its distribution yield is a bit low at close to 7.7% but PIMCO has aggressively raised the dividends in the last two years. The fund is offering an attractive discount of -11.6% compared to premiums on other similar PIMCO funds.

(EOD) Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund:

This fund is selected to provide global exposure as international markets have been performing better in 2025 after more than a decade. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across many sectors and segments. The fund also employs an options strategy to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of just under 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly two-thirds of the total, and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 28%, followed by Financials and Communications.

(EIC) Eagle Point Income Co:

EIC is probably the riskiest fund in our final list of 10 funds. Similar to its peers, the distribution has been falling during the last year, and so has been the price. The result is that yield is still very attractive at 11.6% for current buyers. The discount has widened to over -14%. With reduced distribution, the current distribution is still fully covered by the NII (net investment income). However, on a full nine-month basis (January to September), it only covered to the extent of 75%, as the distribution was much higher at the beginning of the year. Moreover, it is quite likely that the distribution amount may further fall as the interest rates decline even more, so this fund should only be accumulated in multiple lots on a dollar-cost-average basis.

The fund uses high leverage of 31% in the form of preferred stock. The fund primarily invests in junior debt tranches of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). In addition, it may invest up to 35% of total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities.

(RNP) Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Inc Fund:

This is a hybrid fund and provides nearly identical exposure to real estate equity, mostly REITs, and the other half to preferred and fixed income securities. The fund also uses significant leverage of over 30%. The real estate sector had a tough few years due to the elevated interest rates. But with the expectations of continued decline in interest rates, the sector may experience a recovery in 2026. The fund currently offers a moderate discount of roughly -4.5% and a decent distribution yield of 8.3%.

(AIO) Virtus AI & Tech Opp Fund:

This is a fund focused on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and technology companies. But it comes with a unique flavor of combining both the equity and convertible securities, as well as some bonds, based on market conditions. Please note the fund has a limited term until 2031 (extendable by 18 months). The fund also uses modest leverage and has performed exceedingly well in the last three years. It provides a decent distribution yield of roughly 8% and is currently trading at an attractive discount of roughly -10%.

(UTF) Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund:

This is one of the most popular Utility and Infrastructure funds out there in the CEF space, besides the Reaves Utility Income fund (UTG). However, UTF appears to offer better value currently compared to UTG.

The fund invests in infrastructure companies, which consist of utilities, pipelines, toll roads, airports, railroads, ports, and telecommunications companies. The fund offers a close to 7.8% yield and a discount of just under -6%. The fund offers a very reliable distribution payout as it has paid consistent and growing distributions since its inception.

(ARDC) Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund:

According to the fund:

"The Fund's investment objective is to provide an attractive level of total return, primarily through current income and, secondarily, through capital appreciation. The Fund invests primarily in a broad, dynamically managed portfolio of (i) senior secured loans ("Senior Loans") made primarily to companies whose debt is rated below investment grade; (ii) corporate bonds ("Corporate Bonds") that are primarily high yield issues rated below investment grade; (iii) other fixed-income instruments of a similar nature that may be represented by derivatives; and (iv) securities of collateralized loan obligations ("CLOs")."

Recently, the discount has widened and is currently at -7.8%. The yield is very attractive at 10% plus (paid on a monthly basis), and the distribution record is very consistent. Also, the overall performance record has been decent. However, with interest rates declining and the fund’s exposure to floating-rate loans and CLOs, it may come under further pressure.

(GAM) General American Investors:

GAM is our selection for the core U.S. equity fund for this month. It should be noted that the fund pays annually in the month of December, and the payment is variable from one year to another. Based on the distribution announced for December 2025, the yield is quite attractive at 10.95%. The current discount is -10%.

The primary investment objective of the fund is to seek capital appreciation, with income only a secondary objective. The fund invests in equity securities with growth potential at reasonable valuations. The fund also uses a modest leverage in the form of preferred stock.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks:

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 9.25% (as of 12/19/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 15.6%, 11.6%, and 11.9% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

.

data.title: Hess Midstream: No Adjusted EBITDA Growth
data.description:
Hess Midstream (HESM) has noted that EBITDA will remain essentially the same in 2026 as it was in 2025. Questions during the conference call more than once covered some of the downside potential. This is important because the last article and articles before that have repeatedly covered that Hess, before the acquisition by Chevron (CVX) made no money on this acreage (essentially). Even though Hess Midstream makes good money, if the business it services does not make money, then Hess Midstream is not worth much.

What was also noted during the conference call was an attempt to cut costs by moving to three rigs and drilling longer wells. This was combined with the mention of rising natural gas production as the overall production aged. Natural gas prices could rise as the North American market potentially joins the stronger world market pricing. This is made possible through the increasing ability of North America to export natural gas to that stronger pricing market. That may be one potential way for the North Dakota acreage that Hess Midstream services to gain adequate profitability.

In the meantime, the cutbacks by Chevron have resulted in more free cash flow for the midstream company, which enables continuing dividend increases. As a result, the recent guidance reaffirmed the goal of dividend increases.

But the greater risk is still that the acreage serviced did not provide an adequate return to Hess. This could well mean a decrease in the volume of production serviced if the profitability issue is not resolved. Hess Midstream is not that diversified. Instead, it has one main customer.

For those wondering what can happen, I also cover Summit Midstream (SMC) which is recovering from several years of declining production for being in the wrong place at the wrong time. It is definitely not fun and was not profitable. The company has now found its footing after years of struggling.

This does not mean that the same thing will necessarily happen with Hess Midstream. But it does mean that the risk of principal loss is substantially higher than would be the case for the usual diversified midstream company. That principal loss can easily exceed any of the forecast dividend increases promised by management.

Stock

The stock price already had one significant drop in fiscal year 2025.

There is very little reason to hope for significant upside potential unless there are reports of decent profitability on the acreage serviced. On the other hand, should drilling activity be curtailed sharply from current levels or even cease, the downside potential is likely very significant. Dividend increases are not going to materially impact that forecast.

Dallas Federal Reserve Survey Results

For the second quarter in a row, the Dallas Federal Reserve reported that the industry activity shrunk modestly. The outlook survey results remained negative with considerable uncertainty. This is not a situation in which production growth is likely to occur. It also does not bode well for the budgets that are due to be announced for 2026 by companies.

One of the biggest industry gripes continues to be steel tariffs, which are raising costs (and hence company breakeven points). It is not the only one by far, as senior executives have complained during the fiscal year that the administration does not understand the industry and potentially may not want to. This has been covered in past articles. But the complaints continue.

There are some positive comments as well. But the shrinking industry activity combined with the uncertainty is ominous for the industry's future. This is not what was expected from an administration that has made a lot of positive comments about what it can do for oil and gas. The whole situation will set up another fiscal year 2022 if things do not change materially and fast.

For Hess Midstream, the uncertainty is shown by the lack of a presentation for some months now. Investors did receive guidance, and management does project confidence about the future. But conference call questions about the interactions of Chevron with the midstream company indicate some investor concerns about the situation.

The Future

For the time being, much of the future projections have not changed. But that is really not where the investor focus should be. Instead, the investor focus needs to be on the upstream profitability of the acreage. While that information may not be available, what is available is a one rig decrease combined with potentially longer laterals.

There is absolutely no reason to be invested in a situation like this until any new profitability strategies bear fruit. No recovery can begin until the upstream business is healthy.

It should be remembered that investing is about avoiding big losses. There has already been one big loss in this stock so far in fiscal year 2025. There is absolutely no reason to stick around to see if another loss results. The situation here is not typical due to the lack of upstream profitability before the merger. Therefore, no matter what happens in the near future, this idea is best avoided until there is proof of a healthy business on the acreage serviced.

Summary

Hess Midstream stock is still a sell in that I cannot tell whether the stock price will go up or down. The forecast of more dividend increases is of little use to the investor.

Instead, what the investor needs to focus upon is the health of the upstream business on the acreage serviced. Until now, the indications of that are a one rig decrease and potentially longer laterals to try and correct the situation. In the meantime, EBITDA is guided to be flat with the current fiscal year.

Since there are so many alternatives that I follow that have a healthy business with a decent yield, there is no need for an income investor to take the risk of further activity decreases that exist here.

The Bakken itself is not seen as a low-cost basin (right or wrong). Therefore, it is best to head to the perceived low-cost basins for midstream ideas. Those basins are the Permian and the Eagle Ford. The risk is far lower in servicing basins where "everyone wants to be". Midstream diversification is an essential safety factor as well, with only a few exceptions that I follow.

Risks

The upside potential of Hess Midstream will be determined by the chances of Chevron making the Bakken acreage serviced sufficiently profitable. If Chevron does not succeed, then there is a real possibility of decreasing drilling activity or even a cease in activity followed by a likely sale of the acreage.

An example of acreage like this is the Barnett Shale serviced by Summit Midstream, which I followed for some years and had no drilling activity until some recent advances made oily parts of the Barnett Shale interesting to the industry. That was very bad news for Summit Midstream, which only recently has regained its footing. Note that Summit Midstream also had other places it should not have been servicing as well and so had to materially change the business model to survive. That may happen here as well.

Another major issue is that it is hard to tell just yet if the midstream management is getting along well with the Chevron bosses. A "brain drain" in the current situation is more than possible and another good reason to avoid this situation.

Natural gas prices are expected to strengthen over the next few years. But future commodity prices have both low visibility and volatility. Any such recovery has to be regarded as tentative until it happens and the new parameters are established.

data.title: These Are Some Of My Favorite Investment Picks For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

The market isn't cheap. We all know that, as I have brought it up in countless articles this year. And it's obviously not just me. In general, this has been a frequent topic of discussion among almost every major financial platform.

There's an obvious reason for that, as none of us wants to invest in the stock market when it's peaking. In a worst-case scenario, we could end up without a positive return for more than a decade, as we can see below.

If you had bought the market in the early 2000s, you would have ended up without any positive returns until the market peak in 2007. The market then needed another five-ish years to recover. In other words, you had more than 12 years of high volatility and no positive returns.

To make matters even worse, all of this is before accounting for inflation.

According to the pros, however, we don't have to be worried.

For starters, analysts expect 15.0% earnings growth. As the handy overview below tells us, that's higher than any year since 2018 if we exclude the 2021 surge that resulted from massive QE/fiscal stimulus and the end of lockdowns.

If we get an actual growth number anywhere close to that, it's hard to make the case that this market is in trouble. That also seems to be the consensus among analysts, as the gap between the most bullish and most bearish S&P 500 year-end price targets has hit a three-year low (see below).

Moreover, "my" (it's a pretty common thesis, actually) thesis that AI innovation will lead to higher margins is also visible in estimates, as the net margin is expected to soar to 13.9%, which would be roughly 200 basis points before the last year without widespread AI (that was back in 2022).

On a long-term basis, all of this supports a steady stock market, although I have often made the case that I believe we will likely see attractive opportunities by picking single stocks instead of buying the market. That's due to factors like its top-heavy characteristics, the market's lofty valuation (it is what it is), and my expectations of an AI "trickle down" effect and stronger cyclical growth.

However, this article isn't about any of this.

It's about 2026, and analyst expectations, like the ones we just used, which need to be taken with a grain of salt. Jonathan Levin made that clear again when he used the chart below to make the case that S&P 500 target assumptions for 2026 are likely wrong, simply because they always are.

Based on the current S&P 500 price, the full-year forecast sees roughly 10% upside, which is in line with the market's long-term average. That may also be the reason why the long-term annual analyst forecast is close to 10%.

As we can clearly see, actual results are almost always different.

The interesting thing is that Levin also noted that some of the biggest bears of 2026 were also bearish going into 2025. I think we all know people who always seem to be bearish or bullish. This applies to Wall Street analysts, too.

Here's Levin's takeaway, or at least a big part of it:

In any case, the bullish Wall Street consensus belies an outlook with a great number of potential landmines, and complacency is the single greatest risk. Being in the consensus is easy: When you’re right, you can take all the credit; when you’re wrong, you can argue that everyone else was wrong as well. Sticking your neck out can get you fired in this business.

If the consensus is right, 2026 would be a boring year, and this simply doesn’t feel like a setup for boredom. And that’s why I wouldn’t rule out the possibility that this may end up being a Berezin kind of year. - Bloomberg

Please note that "Berezin" here refers to Peter Berezin, who has a 5,280 price target for the S&P 500, as he expects the market to get an "AI reality check."

In light of all of this, I'll use this opportunity to show you some of my favorite ideas for 2026. While I don't care about a 12-month outlook, as I have often said, I'll shed some light on opportunities that I expect to thrive in a top-heavy market that has somewhat of a valuation problem.

This idea is based on everything we just discussed in the first part of this article and a 2026 Goldman Sachs list I just saw on Seeking Alpha (see below). That article had the title "Goldman's top catch-up stocks to play growth in 2026" and included some ideas that I like as well (some of them I like a lot).

So, now, it's time to give you some of my picks.

Let's dive in!

Some Of My Favorite Ideas For 2026 (And Beyond)

Please note that the "and beyond" part here is key, as I don't usually cover stocks that I only like for a year. Needless to say, I'm inviting everyone to save this article and either congratulate or roast me at the end of 2026, depending on the performance of my picks.

The Goldman list I referred to mainly focused on cyclical value, including housing, building products, and transportation. These stocks account for more than half of the list. As most of my readers may know, these are the stocks that I have brought up in so many articles in recent years.

While I agree that building materials are good investments, I prefer producers of building products and distributors, as these companies tend to be less cyclical. Also, because they are less dependent on commodity prices, I believe these players are in a better spot to generate long-term value.

Goldman brought up Carlisle Companies (CSL) and A.O. Smith (AOS), among others. I own Carlisle Companies because I consider it one of the best capital allocators in the entire industrial sector. It has a return on invested capital of nearly 30.0%, which is roughly twice the S&P 500 average (excl. financials).

It has a big focus on commercial remodeling projects, which is a market I am incredibly bullish on due to the high average age of commercial buildings. According to the company, roughly 80% of re-roofing permits are for buildings that are older than 25 years. Moreover, despite current cyclical headwinds, it sticks to its Vision 2030 plan, which sees at least $40 in EPS.

Based on its long-term average P/E ratio of 17.8, this would imply a $712 price target, nearly 220% above the current price. Although I'm not saying we will see this return by 2030, I love the risk/reward of this company.

Meanwhile, A.O. Smith (AOS) sells water boilers and similar products, which makes it a residential housing supplier. The more housing starts, the more products this company is expected to sell.

As we can see above, AOS has failed to beat the S&P 500 and CSL over the past ten years, mainly due to post-2021 housing weakness. As it trades at less than 18x EPS with subdued housing growth expectations, I think this one is in a great spot to do well next year.

Next are the transportation companies. I'm bullish on transportation because I'm bullish on industrial output, economic re-shoring, and general economic growth. However, I'm not randomly buying transportation stocks, as many have no durable competitive advantages (no moats).

I am sticking to two specific groups, which are ultra-wide moat Class I railroads like Union Pacific (UNP), CSX Corp. (CSX), Canadian National (CNI), and Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP). I own the first and last one on this list.

Union Pacific is currently in a merger with Norfolk Southern (NSC), which also made it on the Goldman list. If they are successful (I am convinced they will get approval), they will be the first railroad connecting the West Coast to the East Coast (and vice versa) without the need to involve other peers.

Although I'm bullish on all Class I rails, I own CP next to UNP because it's the first railroad to connect all three North American nations through its own network.

While some may make the case that it overpaid for Kansas City Southern a few years ago, I think it is now in a great spot to deliver outsized returns, especially if cyclical growth rebounds.

Another stock I have to mention is Carrier Global (CARR). I covered the stock a few times after its spin-off from United Technologies (it used to trade as UTX). It ran red-hot, as the market rapidly realized Carrier doesn't just sell HVAC systems, but builds entire ecosystems with secular growth tailwinds.

On a long-term basis, this industrial giant aims for 6% to 8% annual revenue growth, which means that even if we assume no margin tailwinds at all (meaning revenue growth equals earnings growth), investors can expect annual returns of more than 10%, as its dividend yields more than 2.0%.

Analysts expect 12% to 13% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027. That number could end up much higher if housing tailwinds return. As CARR has fallen from more than $80 per share to just $54 with a P/E ratio of 20.5x, I think there's a lot of room for double-digit annual returns.

And, speaking of HVAC systems, Watsco (WSO) is the biggest distributor of HVACs and parts in the United States. Its supply chain includes Carrier.

Generally speaking, Watsco is seen as the gold standard of successful distribution businesses. Unfortunately, due to housing weakness, its stock has suffered as well.

I think that ends next year.

Going back to the transportation industry (I wasn't done in that area), I'm also looking into less-than-truckload companies. Unlike their truckload peers, LTL companies drive shorter tours and are focused on filling their trailers with loads from various customers. They pick up loads, bring them to distribution centers, sort everything, and then ship these goods to their destination.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), which I own, has perfected the art of LTL trucking. It owns most of its distribution centers, which it has bought close to high-demand areas. As this real estate is now scarce (and expensive!), it is very hard to copy this network. I'm very bullish on ODFL.

I'm also bullish on XPO (XPO), which is one of Brad Jacobs' companies. It is one of the fastest-growing peers that is trying to become the next ODFL.

Last but not least, in this space, I like SAIA (SAIA). It is not as efficient as ODFL and XPO but has the most fat to trim, so to speak. If management pulls it off, it could easily beat its peers. I still opted for ODFL, as I'm picking quality over a potential turnaround opportunity. It just better fits my style.

In the energy space, I like a long list of companies, including landowners like Texas Pacific Land (TPL), LandBridge (LB), higher-yielding mineral rights (royalties) players like Viper Energy (VNOM), and much safer players like ONEOK (OKE), which is a giant in the midstream space.

That company has been suffering this year due to subdued oil prices. However, nearly every revenue dollar of OKE comes from fixed-fee (with inflation escalators) contracts. The fear factor that subdued prices could hurt long-term pipeline demand provided some great buying opportunities.

Currently, OKE yields 5.8%. It aims to grow this dividend by 3% to 4% per year, which would make this a great investment for income-focused investors.

In general, I think one of 2026's biggest surprises will be the WTI crude oil rebound, likely supported by strong natural gas prices, which benefit from the AI data center buildout, LNG exports, and the related surge in natural gas demand.

There are many natural gas players. One of my favorites is Antero Resources (AR), which owns some of the best reserves in North America, ultra-low breakeven prices, and conservative hedges (high commodity price exposure).

I think this is one of the stocks that does really well in 2026 (and likely beyond). I own its midstream peer, Antero Midstream (AM), for income and capital appreciation.

I also have to add stock exchanges. Regardless of whether I'm right or wrong about the macro thesis, I think one thing is for sure, which is that there's a high likelihood of prolonged elevated volatility in both bonds and equities. I think inflation will play a role, the "tricky" government finances that require a lot of funding, geopolitical stress, volatile commodities, and so much more.

Personally, I own CME Group (CME), which almost exclusively makes money from its exchanges. A company like Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), which owns NYSE, generates roughly half of its revenue from recurring operations due to massive investments (including M&A) in software and services.

On top of that, I like Miami International Holdings (MIAX), which is a much smaller newcomer that has to prove itself. It's a much riskier investment than CME and ICE because it needs to prove itself in the futures/options market.

I think they will be successful, and I'm looking to start a position in early 2026.

This brings me to the last part of this article.

Takeaway

As I have written in prior articles, I think 2026 will be challenging due to the market being top-heavy, far from cheap, and exposure to AI risks.

However, I think it's also a market with plenty of opportunities. While I will present many more in the weeks, months, and quarters ahead, I think the stocks in this article have a good shot at generating substantial alpha, especially if I'm right about the cyclical upswing.

That's why I actually look forward to 2026, as I expect we'll get a lot of food for thought and cover a lot of both fascinating and important developments.

So, stay tuned for that!

Risks To Keep In Mind

Every single stock comes with risks. On top of general economic and stock market risks, it needs to be said that it's important that readers do their own due diligence. Because this article included so many tickers, it's impossible for me to give each stock the attention it deserves. That's why my favorite stocks are often featured in articles. Odds are most, if not all, of these stocks will continue to get a lot of coverage from me in 2026 and beyond.

data.title: Toast: An Update On A High-Growth Compounder, Still A Buy
data.description:
Executive Summary

This is a follow-up to my first Toast (TOST) article, focusing on what’s happened over the last two quarters. Since then, Toast has printed two earnings beats, shown solid resilience despite all the macro noise around restaurants, and started to really show its operating leverage. Fintech revenue is re-accelerating on a higher take rate, SaaS revenue per location keeps growing nicely, and same-store trends for its customers are holding up. With those updates and better margin visibility, my refreshed DCF points to a base-case fair value of about $50.9 per share, and I still see upside from here.

Intro

In my first article about Toast, I went into more detail on what the company actually does—it's basically an operating system for restaurants. Plain and simple. The company sells its own point of sale [POS] hardware and various software modules for restaurant operations, such as registers, kitchen display systems for firing orders, and online order management. Since then, Toast has expanded into more back-office modules as well, including inventory management, payroll, and even marketing tools and dedicated customer-facing apps and other suites.

In the same article, I explained why I think the company holds two very strong moats - Switching Costs and Network effects. And I do believe the company is still holding these moats.

Update of The Past Two Quarters

Since I wrote my analysis, a lot has happened in the stock. I published my piece in the middle of April, when the price was $34.29 per share. Since then, as we can see, the stock surpassed my price target at that time (of $39.2) and even reached a price between my Base Case ($39.2) and Bull Case ($59.9), peaking around $50 per share.

So, what happened?

Let’s recap the company’s last two earnings reports. To say the least, Toast’s results came in better than consensus. As we can see, in each of the last two quarters, Toast beat expectations, and the size of the beat was close to, or above, the historical average, and definitely stronger than that of the last 6 quarters.

Operationally, Toast (and its customers) showed resilience. Especially when comparing it to some of the restaurant stocks, such as Cava (CAVA) or Chipotle (CMG), which raised concerns regarding the restaurant industry in the US amid macro concerns.

That resilience can be seen in a few metrics:

1) Re-acceleration in their fintech revenue (on a TTM basis)—this pickup is driven by a higher take rate, which comes from increased activity in the Toast Capital program and improved underwriting quality. I want to emphasize this point about the take rate. It’s a big deal for Toast, because they process a huge volume of payments—about $186 billion over the last TTM. Any change in the take rate is critical, since the margins on those extra basis points are close to 100%.

2) Toast customers' same-store sales (SSS) trend - during the 3Q earnings call, the management said that the GPV per location metric (which can be used as our proxy for SSS for Toast's clients) increased from last year. According to the company, the increase in GPV per location was due to stronger SSS of their customers:

GPV per location was up slightly versus last year due to stronger same-store sales trends in the summer.

And I think that was a surprise to everyone, again given the climate we were (and maybe still are) in of weakening macro concerns, as Toast reported after many of the restaurant companies in the US.

Because of the last point, of the take rate, an increase in the GPV of the company is a huge deal, as this is one of the main cash engines for the company, despite being "only" 20% gross margins. I have elaborated on that in the first article.

3) SaaS revenue per location keeps its HSD growth - this metric shows the true moat of the company. On the 2Q earnings call, the company said that the increase in SaaS revenue per location growth is coming from both new customers starting with more modules and existing customers expanding modules:

Yes. It's -- so it's both, right? We're seeing ARPU both from customers and existing customers. And that's one thing we're talking about is honing our land and expand motion. But the upsell team is absolutely contributing to that in terms -- and their execution is solid. I think over time, obviously, we want to continue to optimize our product market fit across our whole set of products and continue to drive that. But overall, seeing really good progress across both new and existing customers.

This is just making the moat of the company wider, as it makes it harder to switch from Toast's products. And I have confidence in the company to be able to continue to innovate and develop new products to deliver value to its customers.

It was only recently that the company launched its latest feature (and yes, it relates to AI), Toast IQ, which helps restaurants have better insights into their performance, and what they can derive better ones from in real time.

And it's not a specific product that the company is having, but their approach - always innovate with restaurateurs in mind. As the CEO put it in the 3Q earnings call:

Toast was built by listening to customers, taking real problems from the restaurant floor and turn them into products …

Toast's Growth Opportunities Remained

I still believe Toast has plenty of growth ahead of it. The two main engines, in my view, are international expansion—which I think could be huge for Toast—and moving further upmarket into small to medium-sized multi-location chains with tens to hundreds of sites. Over the last six months, Toast has signed at least three deals with groups of around 100 locations each, but what really matters is how many modules each of those customers is using. And this is only the beginning of those relationships, which means there’s still meaningful room for further upsells.

The fact that Toast's solution is good for the bigger chains (not to mention that Applebee's chose Toast around 6 months ago, as I mentioned in my first piece) is strengthening my point on their ability to innovate and solve problems, as their POS was not suitable for big chains/multi-locations. Toast already powers hundreds of multi-unit brands. Among others, the names like Caribou Coffee, Choice Hotels, Potbelly Sandwich Shop, and others.

As for international expansion, unfortunately, Toast doesn’t make it easy to see exactly how many locations it has outside the U.S. But my best guess is that they’ve already crossed the 2,000-location mark internationally, which would be roughly a 100% increase from last year.

So What Happened With the Stock

Toast's share price peaked around $50 in August and has been declining since then. On September 22nd, Toast's decline accelerated thanks to a sell-side report that Toast cut prices on its software products because of increasing competition. Not only that, in hindsight, we know it's not true (again, on SaaS revenue per location, we talked about above). In the very same week, Toast itself corrected their website, and even put up a notification that it was a human mistake

To be honest, it didn't help the stock much, as the real support came after their earnings, which calmed investors.

Valuation

I used the same DCF framework from the original post, but with updated assumptions, mainly related to their margins, as their operating leverage started to kick in.

The new price target for the base rate went higher to $50.9, as can be seen in the valuation bottom line

And the sensitivity analysis suggests we have some margin of safety, and the cost of error might not be that harmful.

Risks and Bonuses

The good - as a bonus, we got some reports by analysts that suggest that Toast might be on the list of companies entering the S&P 500. If it happens, that could push for a new supporting price level, which is higher than the current price.

The bad: it seems that for the past two years or so, there is a heavy cloud over discretionary companies, as there is fear of a recession that is 2 quarters away. While that recession never came (yet), there is always the possibility it will eventually. And if/when it comes, it could affect Toast's stock badly, as we saw it has some sensitivity to discretionary stocks, as Toast's correlation with State Street® Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLY) is pretty high, at 0.62.

And I’m not really a macro guy—I'm a long-term investor. For me, that kind of environment would just be an opportunity to pick up more shares of a quality company.

Summary

To sum up, nothing in the last couple of quarters has changed my core view on Toast. If anything, it’s reinforced it. The business continues to prove out its moats through rising SaaS revenue per location, improving fintech economics, and deeper land-and-expand with larger, multi-location brands, on top of a long runway in international markets. Yes, there are real risks around a potential recession and competitive pressure in restaurant tech, and the stock has shown it can be volatile when the market gets nervous about discretionary names. But I’m a long-term investor, not a macro timer, and I still see Toast as a high-quality, compounder-type business where pullbacks are more of a chance to add than a reason to walk away. That’s why I’m staying with a Buy rating.

Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street on Friday closed out the penultimate week of the year with a solid advance. Trading volume was low following a truncated session on Christmas Eve and a market holiday on Christmas.

The biggest headline of the week was the U.S. Q3 GDP report. According to a first estimate by the Bureau of Economic Analysis, the quarter saw annualized growth of +4.3%, well above the consensus figure of +3.2%. data.title: NXG: Infrastructure Income, Beware The Premium
data.description:
As I've been building out my closed-end fund ("CEF") income portfolio over the last few years, I've been on the hunt for solid funds that build on themes I'm already bullish on, but deal in income.

Enter the NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG), a CEF focused on Energy, industrial, and communications infrastructure firms. It invests in both debt and equity (though mainly equity), and aims to distribute high income to investors.

The fund has a fairly long history, going back to 2012, and has gone through considerable ups and downs since then. Keep in mind that the fund distributes essentially all of its earnings, so total return is the figure to watch here. Total return assumes all dividends are reinvested, so it's not a perfect representation of what investors made (since I assume most income investors will keep the income), but price return excludes all dividends, so it is misleading as well.

Here are the last 5 years:

NXG Overview

Inception 9/28/2012
Price $50.30
NAV $48.90
Effective Leverage 21%
Distribution Rate 13.27%
Dividend Amount (TTM) $6.48
Dividend Frequency Monthly
Return of Capital % 100%
Expense Ratio (incl. leverage) 4.81%
Assets Under Management $194.57M

For folks newer to CEFs as opposed to ETFs, which I cover more often, there are a couple of things to note. The first is that CEFs are able to use quite a bit of leverage, and NXG is leveraged to about 21% currently. This is liable to change at the discretion of the fund managers and does make the fund riskier than an infrastructure ETF that has 0% leverage.

That leverage cost does seep into the expense ratio, which makes the expenses look high. That's not to say that the management fee of 1.74% is not high. I think it is. But the reported 4.8% is not just management fees, and that should be duly noted by investors.

The other major point to note here is that CEFs are able to trade at a premium or a discount at any time and do not have the same mechanisms (i.e., authorized participants) as ETFs do. Meaning that there is no mechanical arbitrage that forces discounts and premiums to abate. Instead, investors who buy at a discount may encounter that discount for the long run, or it may flip to a premium and vice versa.

Currently, NXG's premium is at 2.88%, which is a little high considering that most of its lifespan has been spent in discount territory. Investors considering this fund should very much consider the premium and whether it's worth it to them. I'm likely going to hold off on this fund until it is at a discount again, though it's become much more stable in the last year than it had been before then.

The current premium is higher than the 52-week average, which is 1.06%. This flip from a discount and into premium territory is new for NXG and something to watch. It makes me cautious, though I understand that different opportunities the fund managers present, such as rights offerings like they had in August, can distort how the market thinks about this fund and how it will price the fund moving forward.

I cannot definitively say that the days of -10% discounts are behind us, though it does increasingly look like a paradigm shift has occurred. So invest cautiously in this fund. Wait for a discount if you are both prudent and patient.

Of course, I'm willing to pay even price or a ~1% discount for this fund because I'm bullish on infrastructure at large. America has long had a building problem, not only just the shortage of residential homes, but also our infrastructure at large. There's a current bipartisan push (going back to 2021) to build out more infrastructure and change the landscape of what it means to build this. This is a massive tailwind for these firms, and something I believe is not only necessary but logical to continue beyond this current administration, even if the executive regime changes in 2028.

So consider that the fund could continue to gain and return far more than that 1-2% if I'm right to be bullish here, and waiting too long could be costly. There is no exact science on when to buy a CEF (Z-scores get close, but are not so exact), and so I'm considering that a slight premium may be worth it.

NXG Holdings

The fund itself is laden with utilities firms, gathering and processing construction and MLPs (which are limited to a max 25% holding). Together, these constitute "infrastructure firms" -- not only the ones building the pipelines for energy, but also the power stations that consume that energy. They invest in electrical power equipment, data centers, firms that build wireless connectivity, and even some cybersecurity firms creating digital infrastructure. This is where the next gen part comes in. This isn't just old school infrastructure, but new school infrastructure as well.

That's part of what makes me bullish on this fund: I see their vision of infrastructure not just being old-school utilities and MLPs, but also data infrastructure.

The fund has 53 holdings, with the vast majority (90%+) in stocks. Top holdings include Vistra Corp. (VST), Talen Energy Corp. (TLN), and GE Verona (GEV).

The fund contains a lot of stocks from defensive sectors like utilities and energy, but is overweight on the most aggressive stocks within those sectors. This is key to understand because the fund may look to those not familiar with the stocks in question to be very defensive, but I would consider this moderately aggressive based on those top holdings.

Here are those top three holdings up against the S&P 500 year-to-date, just to showcase how volatile they are.

NXG Dividends

Whether you're as bullish on infrastructure as me or not, NXG investors are here for the income that the fund can produce. Its all-time history is a little jagged, and they suffered a major cut in 2020 along with most energy and infrastructure firms at the time. Instead of having incredibly destructive distributions, at the time, the fund instead cut its dividend. In hindsight, this was a good move, but it should be noted that investors face the risk of future cuts if the macro environment were to warrant it.

Outside that black swan, the fund has kept up a fairly even distribution in the last few years. This year, they were able to keep an even distribution of $0.54/share each month.

Here is the last one year for the record:

One thing of note here is that NXG has been paying out exclusively return of capital ("RoC") distributions. These kinds of distributions can be considered favorable by some investors because they do not create taxable events in the year they are received, but instead count against one's cost basis, deferring the taxes until the shares are sold. If shares are held for longer than a year, then the long-term capital gains tax rate takes effect, which is usually lower than one's ordinary income tax rate. If one's cost basis hits zero, all future distributions do create taxable events but are automatically given long-term capital gains tax treatment.

With closed-end funds, there is a note that some RoC can be a bad thing, as it can be a sign that the fund is unable to meet its distributions with capital gains or income and must return capital to shareholders. This kind of RoC is destructive and is not favorable to almost any investor, regardless of tax treatment.

Despite all this RoC, NAV has ended the year up over 8%, signaling that the RoC is not necessarily destructive. NAV total return over the last 12 months is slightly lower than the distribution rate by about 0.5%, so if there is any destruction to fund the dividend, it is small for now.

Conclusion

If anything, NXG can be accused of running a little hot on the income front, though for CEFs, keeping up the income stream is usually preferred by shareholders even if it comes at the cost of some destruction. I expect NXG to keep up its dividend moving forward and to continue gaining in its NAV; I'm bullish on infrastructure and energy companies at large currently. The tailwinds, both political and private capex-driven, seem to me to be enough to continue pushing this sector at a very fast pace.

Note that while an infrastructure fund may seem defensive at first, it aggressively picks stocks among utilities and energy firms, which could cause losses in a bear market in excess of market cap weighted infrastructure ETFs. Add to that the closed-end fund leverage, of which NXG is leveraged up 21%, and the high expense ratio, and you have a recipe for excess risk that may not appear at first glance.

With that being said, I am interested in NXG for my own personal CEF portfolio, and I am considering it as a near-term inclusion. For now, I believe the slight premium may turn to a slight discount and provide me a better buying opportunity. So I'm willing to be patient. Though, my patience can only last so long because I don't want to miss out on a bullish turn if my thesis proves correct. I'll be watching over the next few months, trying to buy at an under 1% premium.

So for now, NXG is a buy, but with a word of caution around the premium.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: OUSA: Improved GARP Metrics For This High-Quality Dividend ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered the ALPS O'Shares U.S. Quality Dividend ETF Shares (OUSA) on April 23, 2024, when I rated it a "hold" because, despite being one of the highest-quality ETFs available, its overall factor mix did not stand out, and its 0.48% expense ratio negatively contributed to its meager 1.77% dividend yield at the time. Today, OUSA's estimated dividend yield is just 1.40%, its three- and five-year dividend growth rates are flat, and it's underperformed on total returns compared to VIG and DGRO, two other lower-cost passive alternatives I compared OUSA with that day.

To be sure, performance often comes in waves, but since its Index changed on June 1, 2020, OUSA has captured roughly the same downside as these two peers but less of the upside, so needless to say, the strategy is not yet proven. Therefore, while the fundamental picture for OUSA has improved, primarily due to a better mix of growth and value, it's not quite at a "buy" level, so I'm reiterating my "hold" rating from two years ago, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

ALPS O'Shares U.S. Quality Dividend ETF Overview

OUSA Strategy Discussion

Beginning on June 1, 2020, OUSA has tracked the ALPS O'Shares Dividend Index, after previously tracking the FTSE USA Qual/Vol/Yield Factor 5% Capped Index since its inception roughly five years prior. This Index, according to its website, "is designed to provide cost efficient access to a portfolio of large-cap and mid-cap high-quality, low-volatility, dividend-paying companies in the United States based on fundamental metrics including quality, low volatility, and dividend growth."

Key to the strategy is the observation that companies with higher return on assets typically outperform the large/mid-cap value market, represented by the Russell 1000 Value Index. OUSA's primer also lists low leverage, low volatility, and high dividend growth as key metrics underpinning its "quality first" strategy, as summarized below.

The data above is outdated, but it still nicely highlights what OUSA aims to deliver with each reconstitution. The selection process starts by assigning a score to the 500 largest U.S. stocks by market cap based on their quality, volatility, and dividend yield characteristics. This score is used to adjust the market caps of all 500 stocks, and the top 100 are selected for inclusion. The Index applies a 5% single stock cap and, due to their inherently poor quality features, excludes the Energy, Materials, and Real Estate sectors. Lastly, the Index reconstitutes annually and rebalances quarterly only if at least one constituent's weight has exceeded 6%.

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The sector allocation table below confirms OUSA's 0% allocated to Energy, Materials, and Real Estate, which combine for a 14% underweight compared to the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD). Meanwhile, OUSA's top sectors are currently Technology (23%) and Financials (19%) - a similar composition as VIG and DGRO.

OUSA's top ten holdings are listed below, which comprise 44.38% of the portfolio. That concentration level is quite high for a 100-stock fund, and notably, the top five are all low-yielding stocks, so it's clear that "dividend yield" is not a major factor in the selection process. In addition, Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) only started paying a dividend in June 2024, suggesting that the Index's "five-year average dividend growth" screen must be outweighed by the stock's other positive quality features, such as the stock's 23.16% ROA.

ALPS O'Shares U.S. Quality Dividend ETF Performance Summary

Since its Index change on June 1, 2020, OUSA has gained 89.01% through November 2025 - a solid result, but unfortunately, one that has lagged behind its primary benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), by about 16%. In addition, OUSA has trailed VIG, DGRO, and SPY, which have gained 109.60%, 110.94%, and 142.99%, respectively. To be sure, SPY isn't an appropriate peer, though I think that given the similarities in yield and the focus on dividend growth, VIG and DGRO are potential alternatives.

Annualized, OUSA's total returns were 12.27%, and although it's been difficult for value-oriented funds to compete with growth-oriented ones in this largely bull market over the last five years, it's worth pointing out that OUSA has captured nearly all of the same downside as its peers but with much less upside. As evidence, consider how its 0.71 and 1.14 Sharpe and Sortino ratios over this period - key measures of risk-adjusted and downside risk-adjusted returns - were the lowest. Furthermore, while its "worst 6 months" figure of -15.42% was slightly better than average, its 19.95% "best 6 months" result was substantially worse.

Even when extending the same analysis to August 2015, which was the first full month after OUSA's inception, it still lags VIG, DGRO, and SPY on the Sharpe Ratio, Sortino Ratio, and Best 6 Months statistics. I'm not sure this is appropriate, given the Index change, but either way, these statistics indicate OUSA's downside/upside capture ratio is not great.

ALPS O'Shares U.S. Quality Dividend ETF Analysis

Dividend Snapshot

OUSA only receives a "C" Dividend Grade under Seeking Alpha's Dividend Grading System. Underlying metrics supporting that grade include its 1.37% trailing dividend yield, its 0.28% three-year dividend growth rate, and its ten consecutive years of dividend payments.

Contributing positively to the grade is its 10.58% ten-year dividend growth. However, that looks to be an error, since a ten-year growth rate requires 11 years of history to make the calculation. OUSA only launched on July 14, 2015, or roughly 10.5 years ago, so I expect this growth rate to decline as the system catches up.

In addition, OUSA clearly looks weaker when compared to its peers. As shown below, IWD, DGRO, and VIG have higher estimated dividend yields, better dividend growth rates, and longer dividend growth streaks. Even SPY has it beat on everything except yield, so I think the best way to think of OUSA is that it's primarily a high-quality fund with low volatility characteristics. We saw its lower standard deviation in the earlier tables, but in my view, the advantage was minimal.

OUSA Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for OUSA's top 15 holdings, which comprise 57.79% of the portfolio. Please have a scan of the holdings and the summary metrics at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

#1: OUSA's Quality Is Excellent

OUSA's high-quality features are evident when considering its 17.81% free cash flow margins, 6.40x debt/free cash flow ratio, 14.80% return on assets, and 35.66% return on equity. In all cases, these figures are more attractive than IWD, VIG, and DGRO, and even give SPY a run for its money. Recall how OUSA's Index uses net debt-to-EBITDA to measure leverage, so while that's not the same as debt-free cash flow, both use cash flows and aren't as prone to accounting-based distortions. For example, a company's debt-equity ratio can increase with buybacks, even though its financial risk has not changed.

#2: OUSA's Low Risk Features Are Good, But Advantage Is Slight

Investing in high-quality businesses is a good way to reduce risk, but OUSA's Index also screens for total risk, as measured by a stock's weekly standard deviation over the last five years. Earlier, we saw that this resulted in a lower standard deviation compared to IWD since June 2020 (13.42% vs. 15.53%), but only marginally better than VIG and DGRO (13.95% vs. 14.07%).

However, one important caveat is that earnings volatility is extremely low right now across all sectors. To illustrate, consider the following "Coefficient of Variation by Sector" graph below, with CV defined as the standard deviation of actual quarterly EPS divided by the average quarterly EPS for each sector. Over the last twelve quarters (short-term CV), earnings volatility has been extremely low in historically volatile sectors like Financials and Technology, which comprise 42% of OUSA. Therefore, given its sector composition, I expect OUSA to continue holding only a marginal volatility advantage over its peers moving forward.

#3: Growth-At-A-Reasonable-Price Statistics Are Solid

OUSA appears to offer good growth potential for its price. To illustrate, consider these modified PEG (price-earnings-growth) ratios for each fund, using their trailing P/E's and three-year historical EPS growth rates:

  • OUSA: 22.02x / 10.80% = 2.04x
  • IWD: 20.76x / 7.91% = 2.62x
  • VIG: 25.68x / 10.04% = 2.56x
  • DGRO: 21.20x / 5.97% = 3.55x
  • SPY: 27.45x / 14.76% = 1.86x

OUSA also ranks #2 behind SPY on forward PEG (forward P/E divided by one-year estimated EPS growth rate), so I think that sets it up for better-than-average near-term gains. However, it's unclear whether that advantage can hold, since OUSA actually lagged behind IWD on the trailing PEG ratio one year ago (4.03x vs. 3.79x). This statistic, which covers 2022's decline, might be more relevant, but regardless, there isn't anything in OUSA's methodology that directly screens for growth or value, so I wouldn't count on it moving forward. Instead, it's best to view OUSA as simply a high-quality ETF holding dividend-paying stocks.

Investment Recommendation

My opinion of OUSA has improved since my last review, mainly because of its strong growth-at-a-reasonable-price statistics. It's also undoubtedly a high-quality ETF, and although its risk features aren't as good as I would have hoped, I think it will modestly outperform many peers during the next market drawdown. Still, its 0.48% expense ratio fee cuts its estimated yield down to just 1.40%, and despite screening stocks for high dividend growth, that growth has yet to materialize for OUSA's shareholders. Finally, my analysis assigns weight to OUSA's Sharpe and Sortino ratios, which were worse than VIG's and DGRO's over the last five and ten years. I continue to view these two peers as better alternatives, so as a result, I've decided to maintain my "hold" rating on OUSA. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments section below.

data.title: Zalando: B2B Business Scaling Fast (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Zalando (ZLDSF) in August, in which I recommended a hold rating as the near-term headwinds are overshadowing the long-term potential, specifically the elevated inventories and integration risk situation, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am upgrading to a buy since the B2B business is showing real impact on the topline; there are signs in the data to support the idea that AI is not a bear case for ZLDSF and that the inventory situation has gotten better.

B2B Engine

Back in August, I noted multiple incremental data points that point to a visible path ahead for ZLDSF to scale its B2B goals. But revenue growth was not showing any signs of inflection that would make me go full bullish. In Q2 2025, B2B revenue grew ~12%, which was in the past few quarters. However, things are different now. In Q3, the inflection has come. B2B revenue growth accelerated to 15.6% y/y, and I expect this mid-teens growth to be the baseline growth moving forward given the commercial traction we are seeing. Like in Q2, ZLDSF continued to win deals from large enterprise retailers like Marks & Spencer, Deichmann, and Next. These are not mom and pops. They are large enterprises with significant scale. Take Marks & Spencer, for instance; their FY25 revenue in the UK is GBP13.3 billion. For them to ditch the idea of building it themselves (which should’ve made sense over the long term, as the total cost of ownership should be lower) and adopt the ZLDSF solution, it shows that ZLDSF has a really strong product. This could also kick-start a mini-self-reinforcing cycle, whereby competitors of these retailers don’t want to lose out (especially since logistics and fulfillment are almost mission critical in the retail business), and that pushes them to sign a deal with ZLDSF.

What’s even more notable is that the adj. EBIT margin for the B2B segment expanded to 7.1%, up ~430 bps vs. 2.8% last year. This matters a lot more than you might think because it tells us that the ZEOS logistics platform has reached a scale where it is no longer just absorbing fixed costs. It also dismisses my concern about the integration risk, whereby I was worried that the process of integrating software onto the physical logistics layer would take longer and dilute margins. The bullish view here is that if ZLDSF grows in the mid-teens and becomes more profitable at the same time, it could continue to structurally support a lower fulfillment cost model. Per UBS research (sourced from the company's website), the ZLDSF free delivery threshold in Germany is EUR29.90, while many other branded DTC sites require EUR50 to EUR100. Free delivery matters a lot, in my view, for consumers, and the lower ZLDSF can reduce that minimum threshold; it should unlock more frequent demand. To do that, scale matters, and the larger ZLDSF gets, the more it can lower that threshold, so much so that competitors simply cannot match it because the unit economics don’t make sense for them.

AI narrative

The biggest overhang on this stock is the fear that agentic AI and large language models will make ZLDSF obsolete. The bear case is simple to understand. If a consumer can ask a chatbot (say ChatGPT) to find an item, why would they ever need a platform to search for products again? There are merits to this, but if you think about how these chatbots really work, it actually benefits ZLDSF more than expected. For these chatbots to “reply,” they have to source that information, and guess where those are from (yes, from platforms like ZLDSF). The test UBS did using ChatGPT showed that across markets like Germany, France, and Italy, the ZLDSF platform consistently ranks as the top result for fashion prompts.

These AI chatbots don’t just return any information in a given prompt; they rely on trustable datasets, and in the case of ZLDSF, given it has the scale and has been one of the platforms consumers go to for searches, it becomes a “key” data point for the chatbots. Looking ahead, this should matter incrementally more as the world moves into an agent-to-agent world. Here is how I see it. From the chatbot (or agent) perspective, the data it seeks are those that it can easily “read” and digest. For instance, having standardized attributes, pricing, availability, and fulfillment.

This is where ZLDSF wins. While a standalone brand website may look “better” to a consumer because of the way it looks (different categorization, layout, etc.), because of the sheer size of the entire fashion landscape, there are just too many tagging systems and attributes. An AI agent would be unlikely to crawl five hundred different sites to find a "red dress"; it would likely default to a single site that can give it the information. In this case, I believe ZLDSF is that “single site” because it aggregates inventory from brands and standardizes it into a single standardized page. From the chatbot operator perspective, it also lowers the computational cost.

Valuation Disconnect

The two main near-term headwinds I cited were gross margin pressure and visibility on inventory. On the first point, nothing has changed yet. Gross margin was still down 110 bps y/y in the most recent quarter. But I think the focus should gradually shift to EBIT margin instead. For context, in Q3, B2C EBIT was $35.2 million, and B2B EBIT was $13.7 million. B2B is still smaller, but its mix is as small when compared to revenue (B2C revenue is ~10x larger than B2B). As B2B scales, which I believe it would, this mix would shift towards B2B becoming a bigger piece of the pie, and remember, B2B should grow at mid-teens easily (or more), so overall EBIT growth should see acceleration.

On the second point regarding inventory visibility, the recent headline numbers might still look poor at first glance. While reported inventory grew to EUR2.5 billion, my reassessment is that this is heavily distorted by the consolidation of ABOUT YOU, which added ~EUR446 million in inventory. If we strip that out, the standalone inventory is closer to EUR1.9 billion. The delta between inventory growth and sales growth (which was my main concern in August) has also moderated. Previously, I noted this divergence as a warning sign of future markdowns, but underlying inventory is now growing ~8% y/y, which is broadly in line with the pro forma revenue growth.

With these headwinds pretty much addressed, and B2B showing revenue inflection, I think the market may be underappreciating how fast ZLDSF could start to print accelerating earnings growth. The AI narrative should be viewed as bullish and not bearish. Here is how I expect things to play out. Investors should gradually accept that AI is not disrupting ZLDSF. In this case, the unwinding of that bear case could be sharp. Consensus starts to price in stronger long-term growth and B2B potential. A re-rate back to even the past two-year average of ~23x NTM PE equates to ~42% upside.

I used a two-year timeframe because it captures how the market valued ZLDSF before the AI narrative started and caused ZLDSF multiples to compress. Given that ZLDSF is arguably a higher quality business today than it was (the B2B segment is showing real traction), it makes sense that valuation sees a reversion.

Risk

B2B or B2C, ultimately the end demand comes from consumers purchasing, and that could turn for the worse if consumer spending trends stay weak due to the macro situation. I may also be wrong about how valuable the ZLDSF B2B product is. Not all retailers need such a sophisticated solution and may be happy with a simpler working one. In this case, ZLDSF’s TAM would be much smaller than I believed.

Conclusion

I am upgrading my view on ZLDSF to a buy. The B2B business has reached a scale where revenue growth is inflecting and margin is expanding fast. The inventory risk also looks more contained than headline numbers suggest, and the AI narrative is a tailwind rather than a threat. At the current valuation, I believe the market is not appreciating the positive points I mentioned above. If B2B continues to scale and ZLDSF continues to show that AI is not disrupting its business, I believe the stock can re-rate toward historical multiples.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
